rulings.Case Title: GOLDEN STATE BROADCASTING, LLC VS. THOMAS CARTY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Defendant Enrique Malvars Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Under Ccp 583.210
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 25, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT ENRIQUE MALVAR's Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Under Ccp 583.210.(part 1 of 2) Defendant Enrique Malvar's motion for mandatory dismissal per CCP 583.210 is denied. By making a general appearance prior to filing this motion for mandatory dismissal, Mr. Malvar waived his right to seek dismissal on the ground that service of the summons and complaint on him as stated in the proof of service timely filed by plaintiff Golden State Broadcasting LLC was invalid. No case has been cited by the parties or found by the court that squarely addresses the precise question raised here: whether a defendant loses the ability to file a successful motion to dismiss per CCP 583.210 based on the plaintiff's asserted failure to effect valid service when the plaintiff timely filed a proof of service stating that service was made on the defendant within three years of the filing of the complaint if the defendant made a general appearance before filing the section 583.210 motion. Mannesman DeMag, Ltd. v. Superior Court (1985) 172 Cal. App. 3d 1118 strongly suggests that the answer to the question is yes. Like here, a defendant in Mannesman filed a CCP 583.210 motion based on invalid service even though the plaintiff had a filed a timely proof of service stating that service was made on the defendant within three years of the filing of the complaint. While ordinarily a motion to quash is the procedural device used the test the validity of service, Mannesman held that a section 583.210 motion could be used for that purpose and, if it was shown that no valid service was made within three years, the section 583.210 motion must be granted. That is what happened in Mannesman which reversed the denial of a section 583.210 motion. The critical difference between Mannesman and this case is that Mr. Malvar, unlike the defendant in Mannesman, made a general appearance prior to filing his section 583.210 motion. Per well-established case law cited by GSB that long pre-dates the enactment of CCP 418.10(e)(3), a general appearance waives the right to object to all defects in service and thus a motion to quash based on invalid service filed after a general appearance must be denied. Mannesman strongly suggests that the same rule applies when a section 583.210 motion is used as the functional equivalent of a motion to quash based on asserted invalid service. Because the defendant in Mannesman made a general appearance after filing a section 583.210 motion based on invalid service, the defendant in Mannesman did not waive the right to object to defective service. The unstated implication in the discussion in Mannesman about the interplay between the timing of a general appearance and the filing of a 583.210 motion based on invalid service is that, if a general appearance was made prior to the 583.210 motion, the motion would have to be denied. That implicit ruling makes sense because Mannesman equates a 583.210 motion based on invalid service with a motion to quash and a general appearance made before a motion to quash requires that the motion to quash be denied. While it is true as stated in the tentative ruling issued prior to the June 26 hearing that subsection 483.10(e) does not apply to this case since Mr. Malvar did not file a demurrer or motion to strike, that does not help Mr. Malvar because it is the longstanding rule that a general appearance made before a motion to quash waives all defects in service that requires denial of Mr. Malvar's motion, not subsection 483.10(e). Nothing in the language of 483.10(e) or any of the cases construing that subsection states or suggests that 483.10(e) eliminated the rule that a general appearance waives all objections to defects in service. (end of part 1 of 2)302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: GOLDEN STATE BROADCASTING, LLC VS. THOMAS CARTY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Enrique Malvar'S Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Under Ccp 583.210. (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 25, 2018, Line 1, Defendant Enrique Malvar'S Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Under Ccp 583.210. (part 2 of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only). Nor does Dill v. Berquist Construction Co. (1994) 24 Cal. App. 4th 1426, the case that Mr. Malvar calls the controlling case and which approvingly cites Mannesman, contain any holding or language that a prior general appearance precludes the later successful filing of a section 583.210 motion based on invalid service. The sum of all of the foregoing is that this court holds, in a case of apparent first impression that where, as here, a party files a section 583.210 motion based on alleged defective service (as distinct from a 583.210 motion based on the absence of any purported service where no timely proof of service was filed by the plaintiff), the motion must be denied if the moving party made a general appearance prior to filing the section 583.210 motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for GSB is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(end of part 2 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL D. HARRIS VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss For Willful Disobedience Of Court Order Compelling Plaintiffs Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday July 25, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion To Dismiss For Willful Disobedience Of Court Order Compelling Plaintiffs Deposition. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant The Regents of the University of California's ("Regents") unopposed Motion to Dismiss for Willful Disobedience of Court Order Compelling Plaintiff's Deposition is GRANTED. On May 3, 2018, this Court granted plaintiff's former counsel's Motion to Be Relieved as Counsel. In the Motion, plaintiff's former counsel specifically warned plaintiff that the case may be dismissed for his failure to appear at deposition. (Rice Decl., 9). On May 25, 2018, this Court ordered plaintiff to appear for deposition on or before June 25, 2018. Regents noticed plaintiff's deposition for June 19, 2018. Plaintiff failed to appear at his deposition. For these reasons, the Court finds that plaintiff has willfully disobeyed a Court order and the action is ordered dismissed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA FERNANDEZ VS. SEPHORA USA, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday July 25, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT SEPHORA USA, INC.'S Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination. (PART 1 OF 2) Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant Sephora USA, Inc.'s Motion to Compel Independent Medical Examination is GRANTED. A court order is required for a CCP § 2032.010 et seq. physical or mental examination in cases other than personal injury cases or when the parties stipulate to allow the exam. Such order may be made only after notice and hearing, and for "good cause shown." CCP § 2032.320(a). Good cause is shown by providing evidence of (1) "Relevancy to the subject matter"; and (2) Specific facts justifying discovery: i.e., allegations showing the need for the information sought and lack of means for obtaining it elsewhere. Vinson v. Sup.Ct. (Peralta Comm. College Dist.) (1987) 43 C3d 833, 840. "Good cause" for a mental exam is shown where a plaintiff alleges continuing mental anguish and emotional distress. Here, plaintiff alleged in her Complaint, "plaintiff has suffered and continues to suffer...extreme and enduring emotional distress..." (Compl., 3:25-26); "plaintiff was injured...all of which have caused and continues to cause plaintiff extreme emotional distress..." (Compl., 4:25-27; 6:15-17). The Court finds that the parties met and conferred, See Jahansouz Decl., Ex. D (the parties discussed an IME verbally, it was the subject of emails, and a letter). The exam shall be held at 10:00 am on July 27, 2018 at 405 Howard Street, San Francisco, CA. The exam will be conducted by Dr. Mark Kalish. The only test permitted during the exam will be the MMPI-2. See Carpenter v. Sup.Ct. (Yamaha Motor Corp.) (2006) 141 CA4th 249, 260-invalidating order requiring plaintiff to submit to "standardized written psychological tests" to "test emotional and cognitive functioning." The scope of the questioning will be limited to the emotional distress alleged in the Complaint, discovery responses, and deposition. Questions regarding plaintiff's background must be reasonable and limited to only relevant medical history. Dr. Kalish may not inquire into the facts that relate to the termination except as they relate to plaintiff's emotional distress. Plaintiff will not be forced to create or draft any new documents. The Court finds that Dr. Kalish is qualified based upon his declaration. (END OF PART 1 OF 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: AMANDA LOTA ET AL VS. BIG BUS TOURS ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 25, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant State of California's demurrer to the second and third causes of action alleged in the complaint is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to both claims alleged against the State. If they can do so in good faith, plaintiffs are given leave to file a first amended complaint alleging that they submitted a timely government claim to the State and, if they are able to do so, they are also given leave to allege ultimate facts showing that the negligence of one or more employees of the State was a substantial factor in causing harm to them. Plaintiffs are required to but did not allege that they submitted a timely government claim to the State. Plaintiffs' negligence claim fails because it appears to be based on direct negligence of the State rather than vicarious liability for the negligence of a State employee acting in the course of his or her employment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the State is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: RACHEL COOK VS. EOBOT INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: (Amended) Notice And Notice Of Motion To Reclassify Case, Supporting Memorandum, And Supporting Declaration Of Rachel Cook
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 25, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF RACHEL COOK's Motion To Reclassify Case. Plaintiff Rachel Cook's motion to reclassify case from limited jurisdiction to unlimited jurisdiction is denied. Ms. Cook's calculation of compensatory damages is based on the premise that her damages can be based on any day she chooses for the valuation of the LTC that was allegedly wrongfully taken from her. That is not the law. Ms. Cook's compensatory damages must be based on the value of the LTC on the day she lost them. Using that valuation date, it is a legal certainty that Ms. Cook's compensatory damages will not exceed $25,000. Ms. Cook has not shown that she has any prospect of obtaining any punitive damages. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Franco's Motion to Compel Further Responses to RFA and FI 217.0 is GRANTED and DENIED as follows. RFA 1: Defendant's objections "assumes facts not in evidence," "lacks any time period or foundational facts," "calls for speculation," vague and ambiguous, "calls for a legal conclusion," and argumentative are overruled. Defendant argues in its Opposition that "Its inquiry never reached this step as VDS never received information sufficient to discern that Plaintiff's family had a 'serious health condition' for purposes of this statute." (Opp., 6:21-22). If Defendant takes the position that its inquiry never "reached this step," defendant has sufficient information to answer the RFA as to whether or not plaintiff was entitled to California Family Rights Leave. 4: Defendant's objections "assumes facts not in evidence," "lacks any time period or foundational facts," "calls for speculation," "presupposes¿", vague and ambiguous are overruled. Defendant shall admit or deny this request. 5: Defendant's objections "assumes facts not in evidence," "lacks any time period or foundational facts," "calls for speculation," "presupposes¿," and argumentative are overruled. Defendant shall admit or deny this request. 6: Defendant's objections "assumes facts not in evidence," "calls for speculation," "presupposes¿", argumentative are overruled. Defendant shall admit or deny this request. 13, 14: Defendant's objection that the request is "vague and ambiguous¿" is overruled. Defendant's attempt to change the question is stricken as improper. Defendant provided a denial of these requests. Therefore, no further responses are ordered. Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to Form Interrogatory 217.0 is DENIED. Verified amended responses to the RFAs shall be served within two weeks of notice of this order. All requests for sanctions are denied. (END OF PART 1 OF 2) =(302/JPT)
